speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is May 29, 1996. The survivor is H Henry Sinason. Interviewer, Maureen Halpert. The city-state is Laguna Hills, California, United States of America. Language, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Maureen-- that's M-A-U-R-E-E-N-- Halpert-- H-A-L-P as in Paul E-R-T. I'm interviewing today Henry Sinason. And we are in Laguna Hills, California, USA. The language is English. It's May 29th, 1996. Can you give me your full name and spell it for me, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is H Henry Sinason. Last name is spelled S as in Sam I-N as in Nancy A-S-O-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you spell Henry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, excuse me. H-E-N-R-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you give me the city and country where you were born and spell both, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Berlin-- B-E-R-L-I-N, the capital of Germany. G-E-R-M-A-N-Y.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, what date were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on August 26, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you used any other names? If so, please spell those.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I really have not used any other names, although my name at birth was spelled slightly differently. It had a German spelling. The last syllable-- son, S-O-N-- was spelled the German way, S-O-H-N. I had this changed when I became an American citizen. And I used my first initial, which I use silently now-- H Henry-- because my name at birth and in Germany was Heinz-- H-E-I-N-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am 70 and 1/2.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me again where were you born and what was-- what was your house like?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. I was born in Berlin, which was then, as it is today, the capital of Germany. Both my parents were born in Germany. So were my grandparents, and our family goes back, according to some genealogical work that was done on my mother's side till about 1730 in Germany and on my father's side, as far as we could trace it, to the early 1840s. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We lived in the center of Berlin. My parents were upper middle class. My father [BACKGROUND NOISE] was a sales representative--

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: --for a large textile mill.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name? Your father's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Harry. His first name was Harry-- H-A-R-R-Y-- Sinason. And, as I said, he was a sales representative in Berlin for a large textile mill located in the south of Germany. And he would sell textiles to the dress manufacturers who were concentrated in Berlin. There was a garment district there, just like there is in New York in this country. He earned a very nice living. We had a nice apartment. The ambiance was cultured. We had music. I remember my parents dressing up in evening clothes and going to the opera. We had theater, and they would take us along. We were comfortable economically, at least before the National Socialist regime took over in Germany. I'm talking about the early years that I remember, in the early 1930s, before 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name is Selma-- S-E-L-M-A-- and her maiden name was Allmeier-- A-L-L-M-E-I-E-R. My mother was not born in Berlin but came from a small village in the Rhineland-- in the Moselle Valley, to be exact. And as a young girl, when she was about 18 years old, her mother-- my grandmother-- sent her to live with my grandmother's sister, who lived in Berlin, because my grandmother feared that she would not meet a suitable young Jewish man in this little village in the Rhineland where they lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother went to Berlin to live with her aunt and uncle. And the aunt and uncle happened to have a tobacco store. And to help out a little bit after school, my mother would work as a sales girl in the tobacconist's shop. And my father was one of the customers who went there to buy his cigars. And that's how he and my mother met. And what about brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My own brothers and sisters? OK. I had a younger brother, who was a year and a half younger than I. His name was George, although when he was born in Germany, his name was [? Gunther. ?] Same last name as mine. Unfortunately, George passed away in 1988.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandparents?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What were their names, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My-- on the paternal side, my father's parents lived in a small town in eastern Germany, the province of Posen. That's P as in Peter O-S-E-N. Small town called Schonlanke. This town is very well known in Germany, because it contained the largest matzoh bakery in Germany. And every German Jew had heard the name of Schonlanke, but my grandparents were not in the matzoh business. They owned a number of businesses in that town. My grandfather's name was Herman. My grandmother's name was Rebecca. Same last name, Sinason, spelled the German way. They owned a large general store. They owned a saw mill. They owned a sauerkraut factory [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and generally kept all kinds of animals around, had a little bit of a farm, did a little bit of everything. And I know this because as a young boy, we used to go and spend our vacations there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was Berlin like-- the community of Berlin-- Jewish community and otherwise?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a very large Jewish community in Berlin. As far as I remember, before the Nazi regime took over in 1933, there were about 400,000 Jews in Berlin. There were many, many synagogues. There were many, many Jewish organizations. My father belonged to some of them. Some of them were cultural. There was a Jewish War Veterans organization to which my father belonged. There were-- there was a very strong religious life. There was a Zionist movement. There were other Zionist-oriented parties that had their own program for training young people. And all sorts of things were going on. However, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father, in particular, was German first and Jewish second. Now, this does not mean that he was not an observant Jew. We-- we were what was then called observant. In this country, the USA, this can be translated into conservative. But in those days, the conservative movement didn't exist, especially not in Europe. We observed all the holidays. We went to services Friday night and Saturday morning, if my father didn't have to work. He made sure that I went and my brother went. We both-- my brother and I-- sang in the synagogue choir. Usually synagogues had boys choirs. We celebrated all the holidays. My father never worked on the holidays.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- without keeping a kosher home, yet we observed all the traditions, OK? I think this comes as close to conservatism or more modern orthodoxy in this country as you could in those days. And it was never any problem. I mean, when I was a little boy, it was just traditional at Friday nights and Saturday. And we would have a Friday night dinner, and my mother would prepare it. And we had at Passover, we changed dishes and all that sort of thing. And my father would conduct a Seder, and many relatives would be invited. It was a very rich, full Jewish life. What did you-- tell me about schools you went to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Since I was born in 1925, I started school in 1931 or so, when I was about six years old. And at that time, there was really no problem. It so happened that the best school in the neighborhood where we lived, in terms of academic work and also the most convenient, was a Catholic elementary school. Now, Catholic elementary schools were supported by the Catholic Diocese of Berlin, but they were not, as in this country, taught by nuns or brothers. They were lay teachers. And basically they had the same curriculum as the city schools, with the exception that after regular classes, there was a class in catechism. And the Jewish kids in the school, and there were a dozen or so, were excused from that. And we could go home early, and we did. Now, this was about 1931 and 1932. In 1933, when the Nazis came to power, this started to change, because my classmates, who were my little friends, and we visited each other and played together and did our homework together, what have you, must have picked up new vibrations at home, because suddenly they called us dirty Jew. They called us other names. They would not play with us. They would not invite us. Some of their parents would telephone my parents and say I hope you understand, but if my son plays with your son and his other friends will, et cetera. They knew what was going on. They made excuses, but it didn't stop. It was not an official policy of the school. The teachers were very fair. They never-- this was never permitted in class. There was never any reference in class to anything like that, unless the public schools where the anti-Semitism had become suddenly very rampant. And I stayed in that school until there came a point when the government, by this time the National Socialists, the Nazis, decreed that all Jewish children in Germany had to go to special schools-- Jewish schools-- and the consistory of Berlin-- by consistory, I mean the organization of-- to which of the various congregations. There was a union of all the synagogues and all the congregations, be they reform or orthodox, which worked together for social relief and what have you. This consistory founded a number of elementary schools. There were five or six of them. I went to number four. This was a regular elementary school, staffed by Jewish teachers. All the children were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was about 19-- oh, I would say 35 probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was simply called Judische Volksschule. And this means Jewish Elementary School Number 4.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about before? What was your relationship like between the-- yourself and the non-Jewish kids in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to expound on that, did you have any non-Jewish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before? Yes. We had a lot of non-Jewish friends, people who lived in the same apartment building. We played with the kids. We played in the street. We played soccer with them. We raced our little toy cars together. We played all the children's games, all the boy's games. And there was never any question of what religion were you. Were you Catholic? Were you Jewish or were you Protestant? Berlin was mostly what the Germans call Evangelisch, which is Evangelical or Protestant, I think best translated as Lutheran, was the prevalent religion. It was not a Catholic part of Germany. That is down south in Bavaria. But the northern part of Germany is not Catholic. So even the kids in the Catholic school felt that they were a minority, but nobody bothered them. We never heard any epithets, either against Catholics or against Jews, before that time. About 1934, '35, it started for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the relationship like between you and other students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish students. They stopped playing with us around that time. As time went by, contact ceased. We did not try to play with them, because we knew that we were not wanted. When I say we, I mean for instance my brother and I, who were close in age, and other Jewish friends. And we made Jewish friends through school, and we would play with them. At that time also, the Hitler Youth Movement became very popular, and all our former friends belonged to the Hitler Youth. And as time went by, they knew where the Jewish schools were, and they would wait for us to come up-- come out of school in the afternoon and beat us up. They would what you call today mug us. In other words, they would look for our pocket money and take it and things of that sort. I remember, to give you an instance-- this was later on around 1937, '38-- I was studying for my bar mitzvah. And I had to go to the home of our Cantor that was going to teach me how to do what I had to do. And there were some kids-- my age, not much older-- who knew that a Cantor lived there, because the Cantor was very proud, and he had his name on a brass plate with the title Cantor underneath. And these Hitler Youth children knew that Jewish boys would come there regularly, one every hour during the afternoon. And they were waiting for us. And they would beat us up. And if you denied that you were Jewish or tried to, they would go through your pockets. And if you were carrying a kippah or anything like that, they would really become quite cruel, as children can be, both physically and-- abusive-- and verbally. So it was a gradual thing, which degenerated from just ignoring us to actually becoming physical and making life a little tough.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what about before that? What did you do, let's say, in your spare time as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the elementary school years, well, as I said, we played all the children's games. Sports was a big thing. Sports were organized by the schools. The Germans, both Jews and Gentiles, put a lot of emphasis on physical education in the schools. And we would have physical ed every other day or something like that. The school had a gym. We used that. There were certain swimming pools, which Jews were allowed to use in the city. Only one or two.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that start?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that start?

speaker: interviewee
text: That started about 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before then, you could go to anyone that you wanted. But then the Nazis restricted that and said only one or two are open to Jews. So I remember my brother and I having to take the subway to go to this particular pool where we had swimming lessons. OK? And the instructor was a Jewish physical ed teacher, whom we knew. And all his students were the Jewish kids. There were other instructors there from other schools, but they were all Gentile. So never the twain mixed. I mean, we did our thing, they did there's in the same pool, but we didn't talk to each other. So that sort of went on for a while. And then the government decided that Jews were not entitled to go to any swimming pool, and that cut that short, and that was about I'd say 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, did you do cultural things with your parents or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As I mentioned earlier, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] both my parents liked music very much. My father was an amateur opera singer, and he was active in the-- what they called a Kulturbund, which was a culture-- a cultural organization sponsored by the Jewish community of Berlin. And they would put on operas and plays and what have you. He also sang as a Cantor on the holidays, not because he needed to make money, but he loved to sing. My mother played the piano. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father would sing in the shower, I suppose. And we heard him, and he would-- when friends come over, they would ask him to sing, and he would sing an aria from some opera. And ever since I was a little boy, I remember him singing, and there was music. And they would go to the theater and to movies as long as Jews were still allowed to do that, because over a period of time, that also stopped. And then there were certain films that could be shown in one of the Jewish centers, only for Jews. Usually they were old Yiddish films that had been imported from Poland or someplace. They were in Yiddish. And we were not allowed-- Jews were not allowed to see the popular movies-- the American films that were being shown or the German films. And the theater, again, was put on by Jewish people in these Jewish centers. So we-- and I read a lot as a child. We had a very classical education, even in elementary school though further on when I was in fifth or sixth grade, we read Homer. We read the Iliad. We read all the German poets. We read all the-- it was, as I said, a very academic and very classical type of education. It was very thorough.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your relationship like between you and your family-- your parents and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- we were the typical German family. My father was the head of the family. His word was law. There was no questioning it. He was preoccupied. We spoke to him when-- after he spoke to us. We would not dare interrupt him when he was doing whatever he was doing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother, of course, was a different story. We could inveigle her into letting us do things when we wanted something. And we'd first go to mother and say, well, you know-- and she would say, well, I have to ask your father. And then we said, oh, that's bad news, you know, because he will say no. And she'd say, well, I'll see, you know, and this kind of thing. My father was-- and this is absolutely typical for that period in time. I mean, it was like that at my friend's house and everyone's house-- the man, the breadwinner, was sacrosanct. He was on a pedestal and, you know, his word was law.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when-- what were some of the first things that you saw with the rise of Nazism? The first things that affected you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The first things that affected me was probably the transferring school in elementary school, when I had to go to an all-Jewish school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They simply told me-- my parents told me-- well, a new law has come out, and you can't go to this school anymore. And when the school year ends, you'll be going to another school. And instead of walking maybe five or six blocks to school, I now had to walk for about, oh, 40 minutes or so to get to the school-- this Jewish school. And this Jewish school was a converted apartment building surrounded by other apartment buildings in which Gentile people lived. And they didn't like the fact at all that there was a Jewish school among them. So they made life very difficult, aside from the Hitler Youth waiting for us when we came out and beating us up and harassing the girls, in particular. And German education demanded that boys and girls go to separate classes, but the school was there for both. But for instance, during recess, the neighbors in the other adjacent apartment buildings had complained that there was too much noise during recess. Children who are in class for three hours, after they're released for a half an hour, they get a little wild. And we used to run around and scream a little bit. So the school authorities decreed that we had to walk two by two in a large circle through the school yard. And we were not allowed to talk. And that was our recreation, because the neighbors complained that there was too much noise. And when school was out, the teachers would come out on the street and make sure that we were very quiet, and there was nobody shouting or yelling or anything else. They weren't there to help us much with the Hitler Youth gangs, but they made sure that the neighbors weren't disturbed, because the penalty was that the government would close down the school. So we were very cowed and very subdued in school. And we had to really toe the line. And those were the first impacts that I felt as a child. Now, back home, eventually we picked up bits of conversation where my father said, well, you know so-and-so has been my customer for 25 years. And now he says he won't buy for me anymore. He's afraid he's going to get into trouble. So I lost his good account. And it became more and more difficult for my father to earn a living, because his Gentile customers were afraid, although they were his friends, and they knew him for all these years. And my father had a very, very difficult time dealing with this. As I said before, my father was German first and Jewish second. He had been a volunteer in the First World War. He was for four years on the French front. He was decorated with the Iron Cross by the Kaiser himself. And this was the proudest moment of his life. And he walked around every day of his life with that Iron Cross in his pocket. And I remember once as a big joke, which got my brother and myself into a lot of trouble, we took the Iron Cross. And we sewed it on his pajamas. And it-- it was terrible. He punished us for weeks. Anyway, he tried to teach us that this was next to holy almost. It turns out that later on, this has a great bearing on my story. But my father could not understand that he, as a good German, who fought for the fatherland for four years and four of his brothers-- he was one of 10 children-- four of his brothers were also in the German army in World War I, both on the Russian front and the Western fronts, and the whole family had been very patriotic. And he was highly decorated, and he could not understand that suddenly he was not considered German anymore. He was just a Jew like any other Jew. And when we first heard stories of the Gestapo rounding up Jews and these Jews being taken to concentration camps, my father said pay no attention to that. They're rounding up illegal Poles and Russian Jews that came to Germany, and they have no business here anyway. And he was a bit of a snob, maybe a lot of-- more than a bit of a snob. He was a snob in that regard. He looked down on Eastern European Jews, because German Jews were more cultured, and German Jews were more emancipated. That was his favorite word. And we were really Germans first, and he simply could not fathom that this right of being a German citizen was slowly being taken away from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that the Hitler Youth would bother you. Did they ever do something to you and, if so, what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They would-- what did they do to us? They would stop me on the way home from school. Whether I was alone or with another kid didn't matter much. And there would be 10 or 12 or 14 surrounding us. And they would say empty your pockets. And they would take anything that they fancied, whether it was some change that we had to buy a snack or whether it was a pocket knife or some toy or whatever they felt like. They usually didn't bother the books, because they weren't interested in books that much. So we never lost our school books and things. But they would just take things and then after they had taken everything, they would make threats and say, well, don't come along here anymore. The street is not for Jews. And they would kick us in the behind a little bit, and some of them would give us a punch in the nose sometimes. It all depended on the mood they were in or if they had a bad day or a good day. And sometimes one of them would say, oh, let him go. They're just little kids and others would say no, you know, they're Jews, doesn't matter. And yeah, it-- it became dangerous after a while in those terms. And we would take different ways to school, try to use different streets and different ways to get to and from school. But there were just so many that you could take.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you try and fight them or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Never. Never, because we were always outnumbered. They never-- there was never a one-on-one situation. If there was a one-on-one situation-- let's say you met a kid that lived upstairs in the same apartment building, and he was wearing his Hitler Youth uniform, and he was going to a meeting, and we met on the staircase. We'd say hi, hello, you know, or something like that, and-- but they would never bother you on a one-on-one basis. There were only certain gangs, and they were not kids that we always recognized. They sometimes came from other neighborhoods. It was a very different situation. These were not our actual neighbors. They were kids we don't know where they came from.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop right here and go on to the next tape. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
